source_paper_title: Quantifying Distributional Model Risk in Marginal Problems via Optimal Transport
context:
Section 8 (Concluding Remarks), page 35.

Many open questions remain including the structure of optimizers of W-DMR for both non-overlapping and overlapping marginals, efficient numerical algorithms, and estimation and inference in each motivating example.
title: Characterize the structure of optimizers for Wasserstein distributional model risk in marginal problems
background_latex:
Let \((S_1,d_{S_1})\) and \((S_2,d_{S_2})\) be Polish metric spaces. Let \(\mu_1\in \mathcal P(S_1)\) and \(\mu_2\in \mathcal P(S_2)\) be reference probability measures. Let \(c_1:S_1\times S_1\to \mathbb R_+\cup\{\infty\}\) and \(c_2:S_2\times S_2\to \mathbb R_+\cup\{\infty\}\) be measurable cost functions with \(c_\ell(s,s')=0\) iff \(s=s'\). For \(\nu,\nu'\in\mathcal P(S_\ell)\), define the optimal transport cost
\[
K_\ell(\nu,\nu'):=\inf_{\pi\in\Pi(\nu,\nu')}\int_{S_\ell\times S_\ell} c_\ell\,d\pi,
\]
where \(\Pi(\nu,\nu')\) is the set of couplings of \(\nu\) and \(\nu'\).
Let \(V:=S_1\times S_2\). For \(\gamma\in\mathcal P(V)\), let \(\gamma_1\) and \(\gamma_2\) be its marginals on \(S_1\) and \(S_2\). Fix a measurable objective \(g:V\to\mathbb R\). For a radius vector \(\delta=(\delta_1,\delta_2)\in\mathbb R_+^2\), define the marginal Wasserstein uncertainty set
\[
\Sigma_D(\delta):=\{\gamma\in\mathcal P(V): K_1(\mu_1,\gamma_1)\le \delta_1,\; K_2(\mu_2,\gamma_2)\le \delta_2\}.
\]
Define the Wasserstein distributional model risk in the non-overlapping marginal problem as
\[
I_D(\delta):=\sup_{\gamma\in\Sigma_D(\delta)}\int_V g\,d\gamma.
\]
For overlapping marginals, let \(Y_1,Y_2,X\) be Polish spaces, \(S:=Y_1\times Y_2\times X\), \(S_1:=Y_1\times X\), \(S_2:=Y_2\times X\). Let \(\mu_{13}\in\mathcal P(S_1)\) and \(\mu_{23}\in\mathcal P(S_2)\) have the same marginal on \(X\). For \(\gamma\in\mathcal P(S)\), let \(\gamma_{13}\) and \(\gamma_{23}\) denote its marginals on \(S_1\) and \(S_2\). Fix a measurable objective \(f:S\to\mathbb R\) and define
\[
\Sigma(\delta):=\{\gamma\in\mathcal P(S): K_1(\mu_{13},\gamma_{13})\le \delta_1,\; K_2(\mu_{23},\gamma_{23})\le \delta_2\},\qquad I(\delta):=\sup_{\gamma\in\Sigma(\delta)}\int_S f\,d\gamma.
\]
An optimizer means a measure \(\gamma^*\) attaining the supremum in \(I_D(\delta)\) or \(I(\delta)\) (when existence holds).
problem_definition_latex: Problem: Characterize the structure of optimizers \(\gamma^*\) for the marginal Wasserstein DMR problems \(I_D(\delta)\) and \(I(\delta)\). In particular, for given data \((S_1,S_2,\mu_1,\mu_2,c_1,c_2,g,\delta)\) (non-overlapping) and \((Y_1,Y_2,X,\mu_{13},\mu_{23},c_1,c_2,f,\delta)\) (overlapping), determine verifiable conditions under which an optimizer exists and describe its qualitative form (e.g., whether \(\gamma^*\) can be chosen to be supported on a low-dimensional set, induced by a Monge map, Markov kernel/disintegration structure, extremal coupling, or satisfies complementary slackness conditions derived from the strong dual).
latex:
Let \((S_1,d_{S_1})\) and \((S_2,d_{S_2})\) be Polish metric spaces. Let \(\mu_1\in \mathcal P(S_1)\) and \(\mu_2\in \mathcal P(S_2)\) be reference probability measures. Let \(c_1:S_1\times S_1\to \mathbb R_+\cup\{\infty\}\) and \(c_2:S_2\times S_2\to \mathbb R_+\cup\{\infty\}\) be measurable cost functions with \(c_\ell(s,s')=0\) iff \(s=s'\). For \(\nu,\nu'\in\mathcal P(S_\ell)\), define the optimal transport cost
\[
K_\ell(\nu,\nu'):=\inf_{\pi\in\Pi(\nu,\nu')}\int_{S_\ell\times S_\ell} c_\ell\,d\pi,
\]
where \(\Pi(\nu,\nu')\) is the set of couplings of \(\nu\) and \(\nu'\).
Let \(V:=S_1\times S_2\). For \(\gamma\in\mathcal P(V)\), let \(\gamma_1\) and \(\gamma_2\) be its marginals on \(S_1\) and \(S_2\). Fix a measurable objective \(g:V\to\mathbb R\). For a radius vector \(\delta=(\delta_1,\delta_2)\in\mathbb R_+^2\), define the marginal Wasserstein uncertainty set
\[
\Sigma_D(\delta):=\{\gamma\in\mathcal P(V): K_1(\mu_1,\gamma_1)\le \delta_1,\; K_2(\mu_2,\gamma_2)\le \delta_2\}.
\]
Define the Wasserstein distributional model risk in the non-overlapping marginal problem as
\[
I_D(\delta):=\sup_{\gamma\in\Sigma_D(\delta)}\int_V g\,d\gamma.
\]
For overlapping marginals, let \(Y_1,Y_2,X\) be Polish spaces, \(S:=Y_1\times Y_2\times X\), \(S_1:=Y_1\times X\), \(S_2:=Y_2\times X\). Let \(\mu_{13}\in\mathcal P(S_1)\) and \(\mu_{23}\in\mathcal P(S_2)\) have the same marginal on \(X\). For \(\gamma\in\mathcal P(S)\), let \(\gamma_{13}\) and \(\gamma_{23}\) denote its marginals on \(S_1\) and \(S_2\). Fix a measurable objective \(f:S\to\mathbb R\) and define
\[
\Sigma(\delta):=\{\gamma\in\mathcal P(S): K_1(\mu_{13},\gamma_{13})\le \delta_1,\; K_2(\mu_{23},\gamma_{23})\le \delta_2\},\qquad I(\delta):=\sup_{\gamma\in\Sigma(\delta)}\int_S f\,d\gamma.
\]
An optimizer means a measure \(\gamma^*\) attaining the supremum in \(I_D(\delta)\) or \(I(\delta)\) (when existence holds).

Problem:
Problem: Characterize the structure of optimizers \(\gamma^*\) for the marginal Wasserstein DMR problems \(I_D(\delta)\) and \(I(\delta)\). In particular, for given data \((S_1,S_2,\mu_1,\mu_2,c_1,c_2,g,\delta)\) (non-overlapping) and \((Y_1,Y_2,X,\mu_{13},\mu_{23},c_1,c_2,f,\delta)\) (overlapping), determine verifiable conditions under which an optimizer exists and describe its qualitative form (e.g., whether \(\gamma^*\) can be chosen to be supported on a low-dimensional set, induced by a Monge map, Markov kernel/disintegration structure, extremal coupling, or satisfies complementary slackness conditions derived from the strong dual).
open_problem_quote: Many open questions remain including the structure of optimizers of W-DMR for both non-overlapping and overlapping marginals, efficient numerical algorithms, and estimation and inference in each motivating example.

verification.notice: This entry was generated from the paper text only. It has not been independently verified as still open. If you believe the question is solved or incorrectly stated, please contact the maintainers.
